cases.title: Sepsis (respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 60-year-old is brought to ED with febrile and tachycardic.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.explanation: Sepsis commonly causes respiratory alkalosis due to hyperventilation.

cases.title: Salicylate toxicity (mixed respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA)
cases.clinical_stem: 34-year-old presents confused, tachypnoeic, and febrile with ringing in the ears.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

expected_compensation.note: Low PaCO2 and low HCO3 are due to two primary processes: respiratory alkalosis and HAGMA

cases.learning_objective: Recognise the mixed respiratory alkalosis and high anion gap metabolic acidosis of salicylate toxicity
cases.explanation: Both PaCO2 and HCO3 are low. This is not explained by a single primary disorder alone. The low PaCO2 indicates a respiratory alkalosis, while the low HCO3 with raised anion gap (22.0) indicates a high anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is a mixed respiratory alkalosis and metabolic acidosis, classic for salicylate toxicity.

cases.title: DKA with vomiting (mixed metabolic disorder)
cases.clinical_stem: 28-year-old attends ED with with reduced oral intake and with deep rapid breathing. Associated features include with type 1 diabetes and with persistent vomiting.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

questions_flow.label: Additional metabolic disorder
questions_flow.prompt: Is an additional metabolic disorder present?

cases.title: Opioid toxicity (acute respiratory acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 40-year-old presents with low GCS and hypoventilation after being found collapsed at home.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory acidosis due to hypoventilation with appropriate compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH = acidaemia. High PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory acidosis. For acute respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 is ~26.2; measured 25.2 is appropriate acute compensation. This pattern fits acute hypoventilation, such as opioid toxicity.

cases.title: Panic / hyperventilation (acute respiratory alkalosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 26-year-old presents with acute dyspnoea, dizziness, and hand paraesthesiae with a normal cardiorespiratory examination.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory alkalosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Low PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory alkalosis. In acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 is ~21.4; measured 20.9 is appropriate, consistent with acute hyperventilation such as panic.

cases.title: Renal failure (uraemic HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 66-year-old on haemodialysis presents with malaise and nausea after missing a recent dialysis session.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis from uraemia with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH indicates acidaemia. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~33.5 (+/-2); measured 32.7 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 138 - (99 + 17) = 22, which is raised. This pattern is most consistent with renal failure causing uraemic high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

cases.title: Simple respiratory acidosis
cases.clinical_stem: 39-year-old presents with mild hypoventilation and reduced alertness without focal findings.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory acidosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH indicates acidaemia. High PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory acidosis. For acute respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 is ~25.3; measured 25.3 is appropriate acute compensation. The anion gap is normal at 11.0. This pattern is consistent with acute hypoventilation.

cases.title: Simple respiratory acidosis
cases.clinical_stem: 52-year-old is difficult to rouse with shallow respirations after multiple sedating medications.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory acidosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH indicates acidaemia. High PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory acidosis. For acute respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 is ~26.4; measured 25.6 is appropriate acute compensation. The anion gap is normal at 8.0. This pattern is consistent with acute hypoventilation.

cases.title: Diarrhoea (normal anion gap metabolic acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 70-year-old from a nursing home presents with volume depletion following ongoing lower gastrointestinal fluid loss.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: The pH is in the normal range, but the low HCO3 still indicates a primary metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~32.0 (+/-2); measured 32.0 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 137 - (113 + 16) = 8, which is normal, consistent with NAGMA such as diarrhoea.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 72-year-old with heart failure reports cramps and light-headedness after escalating loop diuretics for ankle swelling.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~46.3; measured 45.4 is appropriate. Anion gap is 135 - (92 + 33) = 10, which is normal. This pattern is most consistent with diuretic-associated contraction alkalosis from diuretic use.

cases.title: Simple respiratory acidosis
cases.clinical_stem: 37-year-old presents with reduced consciousness and hypoventilation without focal findings.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory acidosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH indicates acidaemia. High PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory acidosis. For acute respiratory acidosis, expected HCO3 is ~25.8; measured 25.9 is appropriate acute compensation. The anion gap is normal at 10.0. This pattern is consistent with acute hypoventilation.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 81-year-old on regular frusemide presents with dizziness, weakness, and poor oral intake after several days of increased diuretic use.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~45.6; measured 45.5 is appropriate. Anion gap is 138 - (94 + 32) = 12, which is normal. This pattern is most consistent with diuretic-associated contraction alkalosis from diuretic use.

cases.title: DKA (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 19-year-old presents to ED tachypnoea, abdominal pain, and polyuria.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH = acidaemia. Low HCO3 indicates metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~26.0 (+/-2); measured 26.0 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 141 - (101 + 12) = 28 (raised), consistent with HAGMA such as DKA.

cases.title: COPD (chronic respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 65-year-old with chronic respiratory symptoms presents with worsening shortness of breath over several days.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chronic respiratory acidosis with appropriate renal compensation
cases.explanation: pH is low or near-normal with elevated PaCO2, indicating respiratory acidosis. In chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by ~4 mmol/L per 10 mmHg PaCO2 above 40. Expected HCO3 is ~36.8; measured 37.6 is appropriately elevated, consistent with chronic respiratory compensation seen in COPD.

cases.title: Renal failure (uraemic HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis from uraemia with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH indicates acidaemia. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~26.0 (+/-2); measured 24.4 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 136 - (96 + 12) = 28, which is raised. This pattern is most consistent with renal failure causing uraemic high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

cases.title: Simple respiratory alkalosis
cases.clinical_stem: 25-year-old presents with fast breathing and hand tingling while visibly distressed.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise acute respiratory alkalosis with appropriate metabolic compensation
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Low PaCO2 indicates a primary respiratory alkalosis. For acute respiratory alkalosis, expected HCO3 is ~21.6; measured 21.9 is appropriate acute compensation. The anion gap is normal at 9.0. This pattern is consistent with simple hyperventilation.

cases.title: Diarrhoea (normal anion gap metabolic acidosis)
cases.clinical_stem: 70-year-old from a nursing home presents with volume depletion following ongoing lower gastrointestinal fluid loss.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: The pH is in the normal range, but the low HCO3 still indicates a primary metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~33.5 (+/-2); measured 33.6 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 140 - (113 + 17) = 10, which is normal, consistent with NAGMA such as diarrhoea.

cases.title: DKA (HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 18-year-old comes to ED with nausea and abdominal discomfort, deep rapid breathing, and general malaise.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH = acidaemia. Low HCO3 indicates metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~27.5 (+/-2); measured 28.4 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 138 - (105 + 13) = 20 (raised), consistent with HAGMA such as DKA.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise normal anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: The pH is in the normal range, but the low HCO3 still indicates a primary metabolic acidosis with respiratory compensation. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~35.0 (+/-2); measured 34.8 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 136 - (106 + 18) = 12, which is normal, consistent with NAGMA such as diarrhoea.

cases.title: Diuretic use (metabolic alkalosis with respiratory compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 83-year-old with heart failure presents with cramps, lethargy, and dry mucous membranes after recent aggressive diuretic escalation.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chloride-responsive metabolic alkalosis from diuretic-associated volume depletion
cases.explanation: High pH indicates alkalaemia. Elevated HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic alkalosis. Expected compensatory PaCO2 is ~48.4; measured 48.9 is appropriate. Anion gap is 140 - (96 + 36) = 8, which is normal. This pattern is most consistent with diuretic-associated contraction alkalosis from diuretic use.

cases.title: Renal failure (uraemic HAGMA with appropriate respiratory compensation)

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis from uraemia with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH indicates acidaemia. Low HCO3 indicates a primary metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~27.5 (+/-2); measured 29.0 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 136 - (97 + 13) = 26, which is raised. This pattern is most consistent with renal failure causing uraemic high anion gap metabolic acidosis.

cases.learning_objective: Recognise high anion gap metabolic acidosis with appropriate respiratory compensation
cases.explanation: Low pH = acidaemia. Low HCO3 indicates metabolic acidosis. Winter's formula predicts PaCO2 ~23.0 (+/-2); measured 22.6 is appropriate compensation. Anion gap is 134 - (96 + 10) = 28 (raised), consistent with HAGMA such as DKA.

cases.title: Salicylate toxicity (mixed respiratory alkalosis + HAGMA)
cases.clinical_stem: 21-year-old presents with tinnitus, vomiting, fever, and rapid breathing after an overdose.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

questions_flow.label: Anion gap
questions_flow.prompt: What is the anion gap status?

expected_compensation.note: Low PaCO2 and low HCO3 are due to two primary processes: respiratory alkalosis and HAGMA

cases.learning_objective: Recognise the mixed respiratory alkalosis and high anion gap metabolic acidosis of salicylate toxicity
cases.explanation: Both PaCO2 and HCO3 are low. This is not explained by a single primary disorder alone. The low PaCO2 indicates a respiratory alkalosis, while the low HCO3 with raised anion gap (25.0) indicates a high anion gap metabolic acidosis. This is a mixed respiratory alkalosis and metabolic acidosis, classic for salicylate toxicity.

cases.title: COPD (chronic respiratory acidosis with metabolic compensation)
cases.clinical_stem: 68-year-old smoker presents with worsening dyspnoea, productive cough, and chronic exercise limitation.

questions_flow.label: Primary acid-base disorder
questions_flow.prompt: What is the primary acid-base disorder?

cases.learning_objective: Recognise chronic respiratory acidosis with appropriate renal compensation
cases.explanation: pH is low or near-normal with elevated PaCO2, indicating respiratory acidosis. In chronic respiratory acidosis, HCO3 should rise by ~4 mmol/L per 10 mmHg PaCO2 above 40. Expected HCO3 is ~30.4; measured 30.6 is appropriately elevated, consistent with chronic respiratory compensation seen in COPD.
